serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dry-braised yellow croaker is approximately 350 g, representing a whole fish portion as served in a Chinese restaurant setting, often shared among 2–3 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, croaker, Atlantic, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for dry-braised large yellow croaker (Larimichthys crocea) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy cooked croaker (dry heat) is used as the closest approved analogue for the fish base. Sodium and fat values are adjusted upward to reflect the braising sauce (soy sauce, oil) typical of this preparation, consistent with published Chinese culinary nutrition references. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Adjustments are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry-braised yellow croaker. Fish and seafood dishes with minimal carbohydrate content are consistently classified as low GI (GI < 15) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. A GI of 5 is assigned by analogy with cooked fish dishes; this is consistent with the negligible starch content of the dish. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (5 × 3.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 0.61, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium, representing over 79% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 5 and only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, dry-braised yellow croaker has a negligible glycemic impact and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids offset by high sodium from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The yellow croaker provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s, which support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from soy sauce braising warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; a reduced-sodium preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry-Braised Yellow Croaker (干烧大黄鱼, Gān shāo dà huángyú) is a classic Chinese fish dish featuring the large yellow croaker (Larimichthys crocea), a prized marine fish native to the East China Sea and historically significant in Chinese cuisine. The dish is prepared using the 'gan shao' (dry-braise) technique: the whole fish is first pan-fried until golden and crisp on the exterior, then braised in a concentrated sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, scallions, and sugar, with the liquid reduced almost completely to coat the fish in a glossy, intensely savoury glaze. The result is a fish with a tender, flaky interior and a deeply umami, slightly sweet and caramelised exterior. The Beijing-style rendition, as served at restaurants such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) in Singapore, emphasises a rich, reduced sauce with balanced sweetness. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 17.8 g per 100 g), provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus, and has a very low glycemic index (GI ≈ 5), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium content is elevated (approximately 520 mg per 100 g) due to the soy sauce braising, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with fish or soy allergies. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free. Fish bones are present in the whole-fish preparation and diners should exercise caution.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for dry-braised large yellow croaker (Larimichthys crocea) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked Atlantic croaker (dry heat) as the closest approved analogue, with sodium and fat adjusted upward to reflect the soy sauce and oil braising sauce. GI is estimated by analogy with cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (cooked croaker, dry heat) with upward adjustment to sodium (~520 mg/100g) and fat (~6.2 g/100g) to reflect the soy sauce braising sauce and cooking oil used in the dry-braise preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4/9/4 kcal per g protein/fat/carbohydrate). 2. The input description mentions 'tender chicken and crispy peanuts' which appears to be a copy-paste error from another dish record; this record correctly represents dry-braised yellow croaker only. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fish dish prepared with plant-based cooking oil; no industrial trans fats are present. 4. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and micronutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked croaker data and are approximate. 6. Fish bones are present in the whole-fish preparation as noted in the source description. 7. The restaurant price of SGD 88.40 reflects a premium restaurant serving of a whole large yellow croaker, consistent with the market value of this species in Singapore. 8. Sentinel value note: no fields are set to -1 in this record; all micronutrient values have been populated from the USDA SR Legacy adapted source.
